title: Watched Video-DYB-22108(hd,16b9,RECORD,)[][DYB]莫斯卡夫翻新CG剪辑02

Viewed When Gukesh won the 11th game of the match, it felt like he was the heavy favourite. Ding hadn't found his footing in the match and with the victory, Gukesh was now leading 6-5. The question was, how was Ding going to recover from this?

Well, turns out Ding Liren went back home, prepared hard, took a good night's sleep, drank his coffee and came to the game in the best possible spirits. Sometimes a loss forces you to give up on your safety net and we saw this today as Ding Liren played one of the best games he has played in recent times. Absolutely flawless. 

Just like his match against Nepo in 2023, he struck back from a 5-6 score and equalized the scorecard with 6-6. As we enter into the final rest day, the match is very interestingly poised. Gukesh will have the white pieces in round 13 and a chance to press. Ding will have the white pieces in the final classical game. If the score ends with 7-7, then we will go into a rapid tiebreak.

What are your thoughts right now? Who has the upper hand in this match? Let us know in the comments section below.

#chess #chessbaseindia #gukesh #dingliren #worldchampionshipchess

Viewed Playing your first World Championship match, and losing the first game - this was not an easy position to be in for 18-year-old D Gukesh. While he could not strike back on game 2, he does the next best thing - gets a comfortable draw with the Black pieces against Ding Liren.

The game did end in a draw in just 23 moves - although there were some really interesting elements. One of the questions was, what does Ding play with the White pieces? Well, he picked 1.e4, Gukesh responded with 1...e5 and we saw the Italian game on the board.

The result may indicate that he went for a safe line, but it is far from the truth - he sprung a novelty on move 9 with a5! This move sacrifices a pawn, and if Black takes it there is some good initiative for White. Gukesh decided against it, and entered a Rook and Knight endgame which seemed to slightly favor White.

Ding did try for a few moves to push for an advantage, but soon decided to repeat the moves and Gukesh obliged. A short draw in 23 moves - while some may view it as a wasted opportunity with the White pieces for Ding, it's also not the best news for Gukesh as Ding leads by 1.5-0.5. This was a "testing the waters" game in many ways!

The players discussed for a short while after the game, and seemed to be in good spirits during the press conference. Gukesh has the White pieces tomorrow for Game 3 - will he go all out, or will he pick a solid approach? What do you think? Let us know in the comments.

Photos: Eng Chin An, Maria Emelianova
